input.prompt:
You are updating the main architecture spec for SGA V2. The file is at `/Users/dako/LocalDocument/200-Project/SGA_V2/docs/00-specs/architecture-spec-v2.md`. Read it first, then make ALL of the following changes:

## Changes required:

### 1. Section 1 — Replace ASCII diagram with Mermaid UML
Replace the ASCII art code block (lines 17-22) with a Mermaid diagram showing the full system architecture:
- React SPA (Vite + Bun) connecting via SSE/POST to FastAPI
- FastAPI containing: AI layer (orchestration loop, prompt assembly, context manager, schema coercion), API endpoints (chat SSE, session CRUD, saved content CRUD, auth)
- 7 tool handlers connecting to SQLite KB
- SQLite KB (read-only: recipes, PCSV mappings, products, substitutions, glossary)
- PostgreSQL (mutable: sessions, users/profiles, saved content)
- OpenRouter → Claude (external LLM)
- Show the data flow direction

Keep the ASCII version as a comment or remove it — the Mermaid is the replacement.

### 2. Section 4 (line 75): "six tools" → "seven tools"

### 3. Section 5 (lines 93-137): Tool Design
- Change title from "Six tools" to "Seven tools"
- Add section 5.7 for `translate_term`:
  - Params: `term`, `direction?: "en_to_zh" | "zh_to_en" | "auto"`
  - Returns: `{term, translation, direction, match_type: "exact" | "partial" | "none"}`
  - Executes: Lookup against glossary table in SQLite. Covers ingredient names, cooking terms, grocery terms.
- In Design notes:
  - Replace the fridge recall line ("No `get_user_history` tool — fridge recall (OQ-1 in product spec) is deferred...") with: "Fridge recall (OQ-1) — removed from scope. The system relies on what users tell it in each session, not purchase history tracking."
  - Remove the line about "If Phase 1 testing shows the LLM struggles with 6 tools, merge..." — this is confirmed at 7 tools, the hedge is no longer needed.

### 4. Section 9 (line 245): Screen-agnostic API
Change the sentence about the `screen` field. Currently says: "The `screen` field in the request body tells the backend what event types to emit." 
Change to: "The `screen` field in the request body is a context hint for the backend (used for context compression and logging), not a directive for which event types to emit. The backend always emits all relevant event types; the frontend decides what to render based on the current screen."

### 5. Section 10: Add mock auth note
After the "Future" paragraph, add:
"**Phase 2a (local development):** Mock auth — a hardcoded dev user with a pre-issued JWT. The full API contract (Bearer token on all requests, JWT middleware) is real; only the email verification step is stubbed. This lets frontend and backend develop against the real auth contract without an email service dependency."

### 6. Section 11: Frontend Architecture (MAJOR UPDATE)
- Change title from "## 11. Frontend Architecture (Suggested — Not Confirmed)" to "## 11. Frontend Architecture"
- Update tech description to: "React SPA with SSE client, screen-based state machine, and component library."
- Update the component tree to show saved content as full-screen detail views accessed from sidebar:
```
App
├── SSEClient          → singleton, manages EventSource connection
├── SessionState       → accumulated SSE events → typed state (useReducer + Context)
├── Screens (core flow)
│   ├── Home           → text input, quick-start chips, no SSE
│   ├── Clarify        → PCV indicators, clarify questions, chat input
│   ├── Recipes        → recipe cards, swap interaction, chat input
│   └── Grocery        → store-grouped checklist, no chat input
├── Screens (saved content — full-screen detail, accessed from Sidebar)
│   ├── SavedMealPlan  → expandable recipe list, add/remove, chat input
│   ├── SavedRecipe    → editable recipe card, chat input
│   └── SavedGroceryList → checklist with add/remove, copy to clipboard
├── Sidebar            → saved content index (meal plans, recipes, grocery lists)
├── ChatInput          → shared component on Clarify, Recipes, and saved content screens
└── InfoSheet          → bottom sheet for recipe info (bilingual name, flavor tags, description)
```
- Update Tech line to: "**Tech:** React + TypeScript, Vite, Bun (package manager), shadcn/ui (component primitives), Tailwind CSS (styling — Soft Bento design tokens mapped to Tailwind config), `useReducer` + Context (state management). No meta-framework — single-page app doesn't need SSR."

### 7. Section 12: Deployment (REWRITE)
Replace the entire section with a phased deployment approach:

Title: "## 12. Deployment"

**Phased approach: local-first, scale when needed.**

Phase 2a (local development):
```
Docker Compose (local)
├── fastapi-app (backend + SQLite KB bundled)
├── postgres (sessions, saved content, users)
└── Frontend: Vite dev server (hot reload, proxied to backend)
```

Phase 2b (single-instance deployment):
```
Cloud host (Render / DigitalOcean App Platform)
├── Web service: FastAPI (backend + SQLite KB bundled)
├── Managed PostgreSQL
└── Frontend: static files served by CDN or same host
```

Phase 3 (AWS, scalable):
```
AWS
├── ECS Fargate (FastAPI containers, auto-scaling)
│   └── SQLite KB bundled per container (read-only, no shared state)
├── RDS PostgreSQL (managed, connection pooling via PgBouncer)
├── ALB (Application Load Balancer — native SSE support, sticky sessions if needed)
├── S3 + CloudFront (frontend static files)
└── ElastiCache Redis (rate limiting, session cache — added when needed)
```

Keep the "Scale-ready by design" points. Keep the "Not chosen" section. Update "Future exploration" to reference AWS-specific load testing.

### 8. Phase Alignment table: Update these rows:
- Auth: "Not needed" | "Mock auth (dev JWT)" | "Magic link + JWT" (move magic link to Phase 3, add "OAuth providers" as Phase 3+)
- Deployment: "Claude artifact" | "Local Docker Compose" | "AWS (ECS/Fargate + RDS)"
- Frontend: "Not needed" | "React SPA (shadcn/ui + Tailwind)" | "PWA + performance optimization"

### 9. Open Questions: Remove OQ-1 entirely. Keep OQ-2 through OQ-5.

### 10. Add new section after Section 12 (before Phase Alignment):

"## 13. Error Handling"

"**LLM failures:** If the OpenRouter API call fails mid-orchestration (timeout, 5xx, rate limit), the backend retries once with exponential backoff. If the retry fails, return partial results — whatever tools completed successfully — with `done: {status: "partial", reason: "llm_error"}`. The frontend shows what it has plus a retry button."

"**Tool handler failures:** If a tool handler throws (bad SQL, missing data), the error is returned to the LLM as a tool result: `{error: "..."}`. The LLM can reason about the failure and adjust (skip that tool, try different parameters, or explain the limitation to the user). This avoids aborting the entire orchestration for a single tool failure."

"**SSE connection drops:** The `done` event carries a status field. If the client reconnects mid-stream, `GET /session/{id}` returns the current session state so the frontend can rebuild from the last checkpoint."

### 11. Add new section:

"## 14. Mobile Strategy"

"**PWA-first.** The React SPA gets a service worker and web app manifest, making it installable on mobile home screens. This provides a native-like experience (full screen, no browser chrome) without a separate codebase."

"**React Native (deferred).** If PWA limitations become blocking (push notifications, native gestures), a React Native app consuming the same API is the upgrade path. The screen-agnostic API design ensures the backend requires no changes."

Make sure the section numbering is correct after adding new sections. The Phase Alignment table and Open Questions should come after all numbered sections.

Do ALL edits. Do not skip any.

input.prompt: Make edits to two files. Read each file first before editing. ## File 1: `/Users/dako/LocalDocument/200-Project/SGA_V2/docs/02-notes/architecture-decision-records.md` 1. Find ADR-14 (around line 289). At the end of the ADR-14 section (before the `---` separator that starts ADR-15), add this update note: ``` > **Phase 3 target:** AWS (ECS/Fargate for backend containers, RDS for managed PostgreSQL, ALB for SSE-compatible load balancing, S3 + CloudFront for frontend). The Docker Compose service structure maps directly to ECS task definitions — no architectural rework needed. ``` 2. After ADR-15 (at the end of the ADRs, before the References section), add a new ADR-16: ``` --- ## ADR-16: Frontend Stack — React + Vite + shadcn/ui + Tailwind + Bun **Decision:** Use React + TypeScript with Vite as the build tool, Bun as the package manager, shadcn/ui for component primitives, and Tailwind CSS for styling. Design tokens from the Soft Bento design system are mapped into the Tailwind configuration. **Why:** Solo developer needs to minimize frontend time while maintaining quality. shadcn/ui provides accessible, well-structured component primitives (Card, Button, Dialog, Input) that can be customized via Tailwind. Bun replaces npm with 10-30x faster installs. Tailwind's utility-first approach maps naturally to extracting design tokens from the Soft Bento reference into `tailwind.config.ts`. No meta-framework (Next.js) needed — the app is a client-side SPA with no SSR requirements. **Why not alternatives:** - *Next.js:* Adds SSR complexity and a redundant server layer when FastAPI is already the backend. SSE proxying through Next.js adds friction. - *Vue/Svelte:* Smaller ecosystems. No mobile path equivalent to React Native. - *Expo (React Native for web):* Web output quality is noticeably worse than native web. Design system wouldn't translate to React Native's StyleSheet model. **Trade-off:** shadcn/ui components are copied into the project (not installed as a package), which means manual updates. Acceptable given the small component surface area (< 15 components). ``` Also update the ADR index table at the top of the file. Add a new row: `| ADR-16 | Frontend Stack: React + Vite + shadcn/ui + Tailwind + Bun | Decided | New |` ## File 2: `/Users/dako/LocalDocument/200-Project/SGA_V2/docs/00-specs/soft-bento-preview.html` Find and remove the line containing "Fridge recall banner" (around line 1963). The line looks like: `Fridge recall banner (Home) — soft callout with dismiss` It may be wrapped in an HTML element — remove the entire element/line containing this text.

Find ADR-14 (around line 289). At the end of the ADR-14 section (before the `---` separator that starts ADR-15), add this update note: ``` > **Phase 3 target:** AWS (ECS/Fargate for backend containers, RDS for managed PostgreSQL, ALB for SSE-compatible load balancing, S3 + CloudFront for frontend). The Docker Compose service structure maps directly to ECS task definitions — no architectural rework needed. ``` 2. After ADR-15 (at the end of the ADRs, before the References section), add a new ADR-16: ``` --- ## ADR-16: Frontend Stack — React + Vite + shadcn/ui + Tailwind + Bun **Decision:** Use React + TypeScript with Vite as the build tool, Bun as the package manager, shadcn/ui for component primitives, and Tailwind CSS for styling. Design tokens from the Soft Bento design system are mapped into the Tailwind configuration. **Why:** Solo developer needs to minimize frontend time while maintaining quality. shadcn/ui provides accessible, well-structured component primitives (Card, Button, Dialog, Input) that can be customized via Tailwind. Bun replaces npm with 10-30x faster installs. Tailwind's utility-first approach maps naturally to extracting design tokens from the Soft Bento reference into `tailwind.config.ts`. No meta-framework (Next.js) needed — the app is a client-side SPA with no SSR requirements. **Why not alternatives:** - *Next.js:* Adds SSR complexity and a redundant server layer when FastAPI is already the backend. SSE proxying through Next.js adds friction. - *Vue/Svelte:* Smaller ecosystems. No mobile path equivalent to React Native. - *Expo (React Native for web):* Web output quality is noticeably worse than native web. Design system wouldn't translate to React Native's StyleSheet model. **Trade-off:** shadcn/ui components are copied into the project (not installed as a package), which means manual updates. Acceptable given the small component surface area (< 15 components). ``` Also update the ADR index table at the top of the file. Add a new row: `| ADR-16 | Frontend Stack: React + Vite + shadcn/ui + Tailwind + Bun | Decided | New |` ## File 2: `/Users/dako/LocalDocument/200-Project/SGA_V2/docs/00-specs/soft-bento-preview.html` Find and remove the line containing "Fridge recall banner" (around line 1963). You are updating the main architecture spec for SGA V2. The file is at `/Users/dako/LocalDocument/200-Project/SGA_V2/docs/00-specs/architecture-spec-v2.md`. Read it first, then make ALL of the following changes:

## Changes required:

### 1. Section 1 — Replace ASCII diagram with Mermaid UML
Replace the ASCII art code block (lines 17-22) with a Mermaid diagram showing the full system architecture:
- React SPA (Vite + Bun) connecting via SSE/POST to FastAPI
- FastAPI containing: AI layer (orchestration loop, prompt assembly, context manager, schema coercion), API endpoints (chat SSE, session CRUD, saved content CRUD, auth)
- 7 tool handlers connecting to SQLite KB
- SQLite KB (read-only: recipes, PCSV mappings, products, substitutions, glossary)
- PostgreSQL (mutable: sessions, users/profiles, saved content)
- OpenRouter → Claude (external LLM)
- Show the data flow direction

Keep the ASCII version as a comment or remove it — the Mermaid is the replacement.

### 2. Section 4 (line 75): "six tools" → "seven tools"

### 3. Section 5 (lines 93-137): Tool Design
- Change title from "Six tools" to "Seven tools"
- Add section 5.7 for `translate_term`:
  - Params: `term`, `direction?: "en_to_zh" | "zh_to_en" | "auto"`
  - Returns: `{term, translation, direction, match_type: "exact" | "partial" | "none"}`
  - Executes: Lookup against glossary table in SQLite. Covers ingredient names, cooking terms, grocery terms.
- In Design notes:
  - Replace the fridge recall line ("No `get_user_history` tool — fridge recall (OQ-1 in product spec) is deferred...") with: "Fridge recall (OQ-1) — removed from scope. The system relies on what users tell it in each session, not purchase history tracking."
  - Remove the line about "If Phase 1 testing shows the LLM struggles with 6 tools, merge..." — this is confirmed at 7 tools, the hedge is no longer needed.

### 4. Section 9 (line 245): Screen-agnostic API
Change the sentence about the `screen` field. Currently says: "The `screen` field in the request body tells the backend what event types to emit." 
Change to: "The `screen` field in the request body is a context hint for the backend (used for context compression and logging), not a directive for which event types to emit. The backend always emits all relevant event types; the frontend decides what to render based on the current screen."

### 5. Section 10: Add mock auth note
After the "Future" paragraph, add:
"**Phase 2a (local development):** Mock auth — a hardcoded dev user with a pre-issued JWT. The full API contract (Bearer token on all requests, JWT middleware) is real; only the email verification step is stubbed. This lets frontend and backend develop against the real auth contract without an email service dependency."

### 6. Section 11: Frontend Architecture (MAJOR UPDATE)
- Change title from "## 11. Frontend Architecture (Suggested — Not Confirmed)" to "## 11. Frontend Architecture"
- Update tech description to: "React SPA with SSE client, screen-based state machine, and component library."
- Update the component tree to show saved content as full-screen detail views accessed from sidebar:
```
App
├── SSEClient          → singleton, manages EventSource connection
├── SessionState       → accumulated SSE events → typed state (useReducer + Context)
├── Screens (core flow)
│   ├── Home           → text input, quick-start chips, no SSE
│   ├── Clarify        → PCV indicators, clarify questions, chat input
│   ├── Recipes        → recipe cards, swap interaction, chat input
│   └── Grocery        → store-grouped checklist, no chat input
├── Screens (saved content — full-screen detail, accessed from Sidebar)
│   ├── SavedMealPlan  → expandable recipe list, add/remove, chat input
│   ├── SavedRecipe    → editable recipe card, chat input
│   └── SavedGroceryList → checklist with add/remove, copy to clipboard
├── Sidebar            → saved content index (meal plans, recipes, grocery lists)
├── ChatInput          → shared component on Clarify, Recipes, and saved content screens
└── InfoSheet          → bottom sheet for recipe info (bilingual name, flavor tags, description)
```
- Update Tech line to: "**Tech:** React + TypeScript, Vite, Bun (package manager), shadcn/ui (component primitives), Tailwind CSS (styling — Soft Bento design tokens mapped to Tailwind config), `useReducer` + Context (state management). No meta-framework — single-page app doesn't need SSR."

### 7. Section 12: Deployment (REWRITE)
Replace the entire section with a phased deployment approach:

Title: "## 12. Deployment"

**Phased approach: local-first, scale when needed.**

Phase 2a (local development):
```
Docker Compose (local)
├── fastapi-app (backend + SQLite KB bundled)
├── postgres (sessions, saved content, users)
└── Frontend: Vite dev server (hot reload, proxied to backend)
```

Phase 2b (single-instance deployment):
```
Cloud host (Render / DigitalOcean App Platform)
├── Web service: FastAPI (backend + SQLite KB bundled)
├── Managed PostgreSQL
└── Frontend: static files served by CDN or same host
```

Phase 3 (AWS, scalable):
```
AWS
├── ECS Fargate (FastAPI containers, auto-scaling)
│   └── SQLite KB bundled per container (read-only, no shared state)
├── RDS PostgreSQL (managed, connection pooling via PgBouncer)
├── ALB (Application Load Balancer — native SSE support, sticky sessions if needed)
├── S3 + CloudFront (frontend static files)
└── ElastiCache Redis (rate limiting, session cache — added when needed)
```

Keep the "Scale-ready by design" points. Keep the "Not chosen" section. Update "Future exploration" to reference AWS-specific load testing.

### 8. Phase Alignment table: Update these rows:
- Auth: "Not needed" | "Mock auth (dev JWT)" | "Magic link + JWT" (move magic link to Phase 3, add "OAuth providers" as Phase 3+)
- Deployment: "Claude artifact" | "Local Docker Compose" | "AWS (ECS/Fargate + RDS)"
- Frontend: "Not needed" | "React SPA (shadcn/ui + Tailwind)" | "PWA + performance optimization"

### 9. Open Questions: Remove OQ-1 entirely. Keep OQ-2 through OQ-5.

### 10. Add new section after Section 12 (before Phase Alignment):

"## 13. Error Handling"

"**LLM failures:** If the OpenRouter API call fails mid-orchestration (timeout, 5xx, rate limit), the backend retries once with exponential backoff. If the retry fails, return partial results — whatever tools completed successfully — with `done: {status: "partial", reason: "llm_error"}`. The frontend shows what it has plus a retry button."

"**Tool handler failures:** If a tool handler throws (bad SQL, missing data), the error is returned to the LLM as a tool result: `{error: "..."}`. The LLM can reason about the failure and adjust (skip that tool, try different parameters, or explain the limitation to the user). This avoids aborting the entire orchestration for a single tool failure."

"**SSE connection drops:** The `done` event carries a status field. If the client reconnects mid-stream, `GET /session/{id}` returns the current session state so the frontend can rebuild from the last checkpoint."

### 11. Add new section:

"## 14. Mobile Strategy"

"**PWA-first.** The React SPA gets a service worker and web app manifest, making it installable on mobile home screens. This provides a native-like experience (full screen, no browser chrome) without a separate codebase."

"**React Native (deferred).** If PWA limitations become blocking (push notifications, native gestures), a React Native app consuming the same API is the upgrade path. The screen-agnostic API design ensures the backend requires no changes."

Make sure the section numbering is correct after adding new sections. The Phase Alignment table and Open Questions should come after all numbered sections.

Do ALL edits. Do not skip any.

# Smart Grocery Assistant — Architecture Spec V2 **Date:** 2026-04-05 | **Status:** Active | **Owner:** Dako (@iDako7) --- ## Context V1 built the AI layer as six separate REST endpoints with isolated prompts. V2 replaces this with a single conversational agent using tool-use, eliminating rigid endpoint routing and enabling cross-step context sharing. This document captures every architectural decision for the V2 system. --- ## 1. System Shape Three-layer architecture: Frontend → Backend API → External LLM. ```mermaid graph LR subgraph Frontend SPA["React SPA
(Vite + Bun)"] end subgraph Backend["FastAPI"] direction TB API["API Endpoints
chat SSE · session CRUD
saved content CRUD · auth"] AI["AI Layer
orchestration loop · prompt assembly
context manager · schema coercion"] Tools["7 Tool Handlers
search_recipes · analyze_pcsv
lookup_store_product · get_substitutions
get_recipe_detail · update_user_profile
translate_term"] API --> AI AI --> Tools end subgraph Data SQLite["SQLite KB (read-only)
recipes · PCSV mappings
products · substitutions · glossary"] PG["PostgreSQL (mutable)
sessions · users/profiles
saved content"] end subgraph External LLM["Claude
via OpenRouter"] end SPA -- "POST /chat
SSE response" --> API Tools -- "SQL queries" --> SQLite AI -- "read/write" --> PG AI -- "tool-use loop" --> LLM ``` **Backend:** Single FastAPI service that manages conversation sessions, hosts the knowledge base, implements tool handlers, and proxies LLM conversations via OpenRouter's tool-use API. **Two databases by access pattern:** PostgreSQL for mutable data (sessions, saved content, user profiles, auth). SQLite for the read-only knowledge base (recipes, PCSV mappings, store products, substitutions) — simpler to seed, version, and ship as a file.

content.content:
140	- **Params:** `ingredient`, `reason?: "unavailable" | "dietary" | "preference"`
141	- **Returns:** List of `{substitute, match_quality, notes}`
142	
143	### 5.5 `get_recipe_detail`
144	
145	- **Params:** `recipe_id`
146	- **Returns:** Full cooking instructions, ratios, tips, source attribution
147	- **Purpose:** Keeps initial `search_recipes` responses lightweight; fetched when user expands a recipe card
148	
149	### 5.6 `update_user_profile`
150	
151	- **Params:** `field` (enum: household_size, dietary_restrictions, preferred_cuisines, disliked_ingredients, preferred_stores, notes), `value`
152	- **Returns:** `{updated: true, field, new_value}`
153	- **Executes:** Write to PostgreSQL user profile table
154	- **Purpose:** Persists preferences and restrictions learned during conversation. The agent calls this when users mention dietary needs, cuisine preferences, or other persistent facts — e.g., "I'm halal" triggers an update to `dietary_restrictions`
155	
156	### 5.7 `translate_term`
157	
158	- **Params:** `term`, `direction?: "en_to_zh" | "zh_to_en" | "auto"`
159	- **Returns:** `{term, translation, direction, match_type: "exact" | "partial" | "none"}`
160	- **Executes:** Lookup against glossary table in SQLite. Covers ingredient names, cooking terms, grocery terms.
161	
162	### Design notes
163	
164	- No "generate_recipe" tool — when KB has no match, the LLM falls back to generation in its response text, flagged as "AI-suggested"
165	- Fridge recall (OQ-1) — removed from scope. The system relies on what users tell it in each session, not purchase history tracking.
166	
167	---
168	
169	## 6. Knowledge Base
170	
171	SQLite as a single-file, read-only KB with four logical domains:
172

file.content:
- **Params:** `ingredient`, `reason?: "unavailable" | "dietary" | "preference"`
- **Returns:** List of `{substitute, match_quality, notes}`

### 5.5 `get_recipe_detail`

- **Params:** `recipe_id`
- **Returns:** Full cooking instructions, ratios, tips, source attribution
- **Purpose:** Keeps initial `search_recipes` responses lightweight; fetched when user expands a recipe card

### 5.6 `update_user_profile`

- **Params:** `field` (enum: household_size, dietary_restrictions, preferred_cuisines, disliked_ingredients, preferred_stores, notes), `value`
- **Returns:** `{updated: true, field, new_value}`
- **Executes:** Write to PostgreSQL user profile table
- **Purpose:** Persists preferences and restrictions learned during conversation. The agent calls this when users mention dietary needs, cuisine preferences, or other persistent facts — e.g., "I'm halal" triggers an update to `dietary_restrictions`

### 5.7 `translate_term`

- **Params:** `term`, `direction?: "en_to_zh" | "zh_to_en" | "auto"`
- **Returns:** `{term, translation, direction, match_type: "exact" | "partial" | "none"}`
- **Executes:** Lookup against glossary table in SQLite. Covers ingredient names, cooking terms, grocery terms.

### Design notes

- No "generate_recipe" tool — when KB has no match, the LLM falls back to generation in its response text, flagged as "AI-suggested"
- Fridge recall (OQ-1) — removed from scope. The system relies on what users tell it in each session, not purchase history tracking.

---

## 6. Knowledge Base

SQLite as a single-file, read-only KB with four logical domains:

- **Recipes** — indexed by ingredients, PCSV categories, cuisine, method, effort_level, flavor_tags. Source attribution field ("Kenji / The Food Lab" vs "AI-suggested"). Compact detail blob for cooking instructions

**Effort levels:** `quick` (~~15 min or less, minimal active prep), `medium` (~~15–45 min, moderate prep), `long` (45+ min or requires marinating/slow cooking). Qualitative by design — a "30-minute" recipe with 20 minutes of knife work feels harder than a "45-minute" recipe where 30 minutes is unattended oven time.

**Flavor tag schema:** Each recipe carries a `flavor_tags` array drawn from two tiers:

- **Taste** (5 basics): sweet, salty, sour, bitter, umami
- **Sensory descriptors**: spicy, creamy, smoky, fresh, rich, numbing, tangy, herbal, aromatic

A recipe typically has 2–4 tags (e.g., Mapo Tofu: `[umami, spicy, numbing]`; teriyaki chicken: `[sweet, umami, rich]`). The tag vocabulary is a flat list now but designed to expand to full aroma profile dimensions (citrus, floral, woody, etc.) in a future phase. New tags can be added without schema migration.

- **PCSV mappings** — ingredient → category lookup. Multi-role supported (beans → protein + carb)
- **Store products** — item, package size, department, store. Starting with Costco Vancouver + local community markets. No price data
- **Substitutions** — ingredient pairs with match quality and context tags (dietary, cultural, availability)

**Vector search:** sqlite-vss extension allows adding embedding columns alongside regular tables for future semantic search. Start with attribute-based filtering (SQL `WHERE` clauses), add vector reranking when usage data shows where keyword matching falls short. Fallback option: DuckDB if sqlite-vss proves limiting.

**Schema design deferred** until recipe source data (Kenji's books, store product lists) is in hand.

---

## 7. User Profile

**Structured profile document** stored in PostgreSQL, read at prompt assembly time. The agent sees the full profile in every system prompt (~500 tokens).

**Schema:**

260	
261	### Endpoints
262	
263	| Endpoint                  | Method     | Purpose                                                        |
264	| ------------------------- | ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
265	| `POST /session`           | Create     | Starts a new session, returns `session_id`                     |
266	| `POST /session/{id}/chat` | SSE stream | Sends user message + screen context, streams back typed events |
267	| `GET /session/{id}`       | Read       | Returns current session state (for page refresh / resume)      |
268	| `POST /auth/send-code`    | Auth       | Sends magic link or 6-digit code to email                      |
269	| `POST /auth/verify`       | Auth       | Validates code, returns JWT                                    |
270	
271	Saved content (meal plans, recipes, grocery lists) gets standard CRUD endpoints — no LLM involvement. Saving writes current session state to PostgreSQL.
272	
273	The `/chat` endpoint is the core. All LLM interactions flow through it: screen transitions, chat corrections, swap requests. The `screen` field in the request body is a context hint for the backend (used for context compression and logging), not a directive for which event types to emit. The backend always emits all relevant event types; the frontend decides what to render based on the current screen.
274	
275	---
276	
277	## 10. Authentication
278	
279	**Magic link (passwordless email) + JWT tokens.**
280	
281	1. User enters email → backend sends a 6-digit code
282	2. User enters code → backend issues a JWT
283	3. JWT sent as `Authorization: Bearer` header on all requests
284	4. Backend validates JWT via middleware on all endpoints except `/auth/`\*
285	
286	**Why magic link:** No password storage, no hashing, no "forgot password" flow. Lower friction for a grocery app. One fewer attack surface.
287	
288	**Why JWT over server-side sessions:** Stateless — no session lookup per request. Works naturally with SSE (token sent on connection start). Frontend stores token in memory (not localStorage — XSS risk).
289	
290	**Future:** OAuth (Google/Apple) is a clean addition — same JWT flow, different issuer. Not needed for Phase 2 validation with a small user group.
291	
292	**Phase 2a (local development):** Mock auth — a hardcoded dev user with a pre-issued JWT. The full API contract (Bearer token on all requests, JWT middleware) is real; only the email verification step is stubbed. This lets frontend and backend develop against the real auth contract without an email service dependency.
293	
294	---
295	
296	## 11. Frontend Architecture
297	
298	React SPA with SSE client, screen-based state machine, and component library.
299	
300	```
301	App
302	├── SSEClient          → singleton, manages EventSource connection
303	├── SessionState       → accumulated SSE events → typed state (useReducer + Context)
304	├── Screens (core flow)
305	│   ├── Home           → text input, quick-start chips, no SSE
306	│   ├── Clarify        → PCV indicators, clarify questions, chat input
307	│   ├── Recipes        → recipe cards, swap interaction, chat input
308	│   └── Grocery        → store-grouped checklist, no chat input
309	├── Screens (saved content — full-screen detail, accessed from Sidebar)
310	│   ├── SavedMealPlan  → expandable recipe list, add/remove, chat input
311	│   ├── SavedRecipe    → editable recipe card, chat input
312	│   └── SavedGroceryList → checklist with add/remove, copy to clipboard
313	├── Sidebar            → saved content index (meal plans, recipes, grocery lists)
314	├── ChatInput          → shared component on Clarify, Recipes, and saved content screens
315	└── InfoSheet          → bottom sheet for recipe info (bilingual name, flavor tags, description)
316	```
317	
318	**State pattern:** Each SSE event type maps to a state slot. Components render whatever state exists — no "wait for all data" gate. Screen state machine: `IDLE → LOADING → STREAMING → COMPLETE`.
319	
320	**Tech:** React + TypeScript, Vite, Bun (package manager), shadcn/ui (component primitives), Tailwind CSS (styling — Soft Bento design tokens mapped to Tailwind config), `useReducer` + Context (state management). No meta-framework — single-page app doesn't need SSR.

file.content:
### Endpoints

| Endpoint                  | Method     | Purpose                                                        |
| ------------------------- | ---------- | -------------------------------------------------------------- |
| `POST /session`           | Create     | Starts a new session, returns `session_id`                     |
| `POST /session/{id}/chat` | SSE stream | Sends user message + screen context, streams back typed events |
| `GET /session/{id}`       | Read       | Returns current session state (for page refresh / resume)      |
| `POST /auth/send-code`    | Auth       | Sends magic link or 6-digit code to email                      |
| `POST /auth/verify`       | Auth       | Validates code, returns JWT                                    |

Saved content (meal plans, recipes, grocery lists) gets standard CRUD endpoints — no LLM involvement. Saving writes current session state to PostgreSQL.

The `/chat` endpoint is the core. All LLM interactions flow through it: screen transitions, chat corrections, swap requests. The `screen` field in the request body is a context hint for the backend (used for context compression and logging), not a directive for which event types to emit. The backend always emits all relevant event types; the frontend decides what to render based on the current screen.

---

## 10. Authentication

**Magic link (passwordless email) + JWT tokens.**

1. User enters email → backend sends a 6-digit code
2. User enters code → backend issues a JWT
3. JWT sent as `Authorization: Bearer` header on all requests
4. Backend validates JWT via middleware on all endpoints except `/auth/`\*

**Why magic link:** No password storage, no hashing, no "forgot password" flow. Lower friction for a grocery app. One fewer attack surface.

**Why JWT over server-side sessions:** Stateless — no session lookup per request. Works naturally with SSE (token sent on connection start). Frontend stores token in memory (not localStorage — XSS risk).

**Future:** OAuth (Google/Apple) is a clean addition — same JWT flow, different issuer. Not needed for Phase 2 validation with a small user group.

**Phase 2a (local development):** Mock auth — a hardcoded dev user with a pre-issued JWT. The full API contract (Bearer token on all requests, JWT middleware) is real; only the email verification step is stubbed. This lets frontend and backend develop against the real auth contract without an email service dependency.

---

## 11. Frontend Architecture

React SPA with SSE client, screen-based state machine, and component library.

```
App
├── SSEClient          → singleton, manages EventSource connection
├── SessionState       → accumulated SSE events → typed state (useReducer + Context)
├── Screens (core flow)
│   ├── Home           → text input, quick-start chips, no SSE
│   ├── Clarify        → PCV indicators, clarify questions, chat input
│   ├── Recipes        → recipe cards, swap interaction, chat input
│   └── Grocery        → store-grouped checklist, no chat input
├── Screens (saved content — full-screen detail, accessed from Sidebar)
│   ├── SavedMealPlan  → expandable recipe list, add/remove, chat input
│   ├── SavedRecipe    → editable recipe card, chat input
│   └── SavedGroceryList → checklist with add/remove, copy to clipboard
├── Sidebar            → saved content index (meal plans, recipes, grocery lists)
├── ChatInput          → shared component on Clarify, Recipes, and saved content screens
└── InfoSheet          → bottom sheet for recipe info (bilingual name, flavor tags, description)
```

**State pattern:** Each SSE event type maps to a state slot. Components render whatever state exists — no "wait for all data" gate. Screen state machine: `IDLE → LOADING → STREAMING → COMPLETE`.

**Tech:** React + TypeScript, Vite, Bun (package manager), shadcn/ui (component primitives), Tailwind CSS (styling — Soft Bento design tokens mapped to Tailwind config), `useReducer` + Context (state management). No meta-framework — single-page app doesn't need SSR.

---

## 12. Deployment

**Phased approach: local-first, scale when needed.**

**Phase 2a (local development):**

```
Docker Compose (local)
├── fastapi-app (backend + SQLite KB bundled)
├── postgres (sessions, saved content, users)
└── Frontend: Vite dev server (hot reload, proxied to backend)
```

**Phase 2b (single-instance deployment):**

```

content.content:
340	Cloud host (Render / DigitalOcean App Platform)
341	├── Web service: FastAPI (backend + SQLite KB bundled)
342	├── Managed PostgreSQL
343	└── Frontend: static files served by CDN or same host
344	```
345	
346	**Phase 3 (AWS, scalable):**
347	
348	```
349	AWS
350	├── ECS Fargate (FastAPI containers, auto-scaling)
351	│   └── SQLite KB bundled per container (read-only, no shared state)
352	├── RDS PostgreSQL (managed, connection pooling via PgBouncer)
353	├── ALB (Application Load Balancer — native SSE support, sticky sessions if needed)
354	├── S3 + CloudFront (frontend static files)
355	└── ElastiCache Redis (rate limiting, session cache — added when needed)
356	```
357	
358	**Scale-ready by design:**
359	
360	- SQLite for KB (read-only, copied per container when scaling)
361	- PostgreSQL for sessions (shared across instances)
362	- Stateless JWT auth (no server-side session affinity)
363	- No hardcoded localhost references
364	
365	**Not chosen:**
366	
367	- Serverless (Lambda/Cloud Run) — SSE requires long-lived connections; timeout management is awkward
368	- Kubernetes — premature for validation phase
369	
370	**Future exploration:** AWS-specific load testing with k6 will identify bottlenecks before scaling investment. Expected bottleneck: OpenRouter API latency, not backend.
371	
372	---
373	
374	## 13. Error Handling
375	
376	**LLM failures:** If the OpenRouter API call fails mid-orchestration (timeout, 5xx, rate limit), the backend retries once with exponential backoff. If the retry fails, return partial results — whatever tools completed successfully — with `done: {status: "partial", reason: "llm_error"}`. The frontend shows what it has plus a retry button.
377	
378	**Tool handler failures:** If a tool handler throws (bad SQL, missing data), the error is returned to the LLM as a tool result: `{error: "..."}`. The LLM can reason about the failure and adjust (skip that tool, try different parameters, or explain the limitation to the user). This avoids aborting the entire orchestration for a single tool failure.
379	
380	**SSE connection drops:** The `done` event carries a status field. If the client reconnects mid-stream, `GET /session/{id}` returns the current session state so the frontend can rebuild from the last checkpoint.
381	
382	---
383	
384	## 14. Mobile Strategy
385	
386	**PWA-first.** The React SPA gets a service worker and web app manifest, making it installable on mobile home screens. This provides a native-like experience (full screen, no browser chrome) without a separate codebase.
387	
388	**React Native (deferred).** If PWA limitations become blocking (push notifications, native gestures), a React Native app consuming the same API is the upgrade path. The screen-agnostic API design ensures the backend requires no changes.
389	
390	---
391	
392	## Phase Alignment
393	
394	| Component          | Phase 1 (Prove)                 | Phase 2 (Ship)                             | Phase 3 (Optimize)                   |
395	| ------------------ | ------------------------------- | ------------------------------------------ | ------------------------------------ |
396	| Orchestration loop | Claude artifact, manual testing | FastAPI, explicit while-loop               | Parallel tool dispatch               |
397	| Prompt assembly    | Inline system prompt            | Skill file concatenation                   | A/B testing prompts                  |
398	| Context manager    | Not needed (single-turn)        | Simple truncation (last N turns + summary) | LLM-generated compression            |
399	| Tool handlers      | Mock data in tool responses     | SQLite queries                             | Vector reranking                     |
400	| Schema coercion    | Manual JSON inspection          | Pydantic pipeline                          | Monitoring + prompt fixes            |
401	| SSE emitter        | Not needed                      | Collect-then-emit with status strings      | Progressive streaming                |
402	| User profile       | Not needed                      | Agent self-write via tool                  | Automated extraction pipeline        |
403	| KB                 | Mock data in tool responses     | SQLite with seeded data                    | Vector search, expanded data         |
404	| Sessions           | Not needed                      | PostgreSQL                                 | Context compression tuning           |
405	| Frontend           | Not needed                      | React SPA (shadcn/ui + Tailwind)           | PWA + performance optimization       |
406	| Auth               | Not needed                      | Mock auth (dev JWT)                        | Magic link + JWT, OAuth providers    |
407	| Deployment         | Claude artifact                 | Local Docker Compose                       | AWS (ECS/Fargate + RDS)             |
408	| Memory agent       | Not in scope                    | Not in scope                               | Cross-session reasoning over history |
409	
410	---
411	
412	## Open Questions (Carried from Product Spec)
413	
414	- **OQ-2:** KB seed strategy — which recipes and products to index first
415	- **OQ-3:** Model selection — depends on Phase 1 evaluation
416	- **OQ-4:** Extremely vague input threshold — deferred to user testing
417	- **OQ-5:** KB schema design — deferred until source data is available
418	
419	---
420	
421	## References

424	- **AI layer architecture:** `ai-layer-architecture-v2.md`
425	- **Implementation plan:** `Smart_Grocery_Assistant_V2_Implementation_Plan.md`
426	- **Wireframe:** `wireframe-v2.html`
427	- **Agent patterns:** [Anthropic — Building Effective Agents](https://www.anthropic.com/research/building-effective-agents)
428	- **Tool design guide:** [Anthropic — Writing Effective Tools for Agents](https://www.anthropic.com/engineering/writing-tools-for-agents)
429

**Scale-ready by design:**

## 13. Error Handling

**LLM failures:** If the OpenRouter API call fails mid-orchestration (timeout, 5xx, rate limit), the backend retries once with exponential backoff. If the retry fails, return partial results — whatever tools completed successfully — with `done: {status: "partial", reason: "llm_error"}`. The frontend shows what it has plus a retry button.

**Tool handler failures:** If a tool handler throws (bad SQL, missing data), the error is returned to the LLM as a tool result: `{error: "..."}`. The LLM can reason about the failure and adjust (skip that tool, try different parameters, or explain the limitation to the user). This avoids aborting the entire orchestration for a single tool failure.

**SSE connection drops:** The `done` event carries a status field. If the client reconnects mid-stream, `GET /session/{id}` returns the current session state so the frontend can rebuild from the last checkpoint.

---

## 14. Mobile Strategy

**PWA-first.** The React SPA gets a service worker and web app manifest, making it installable on mobile home screens. This provides a native-like experience (full screen, no browser chrome) without a separate codebase.

message.content: one question, there are too many document, I want to move some to archive and keep one .md for the future development? do you recommend me to do so? what do you suggest?

content.text:
Yes, I'd recommend it. Here's my thinking:

**During active development, you need to reference:**
1. **What to build** — screens, user stories, quality rules
2. **How to build it** — architecture, API contracts, data flow
3. **Visual reference** — wireframe + design system (HTML files you open in browser)
4. **Decisions log** — ADRs (when you hit a "should I...?" moment)

**What I'd suggest:**

### Keep active (4 files + 2 HTML)
| File | Why |
|---|---|
| `architecture-spec-v2.md` | Your primary dev reference — just updated, covers everything from system shape to deployment |
| `product-spec-v2.md` | User stories, screen details, quality boundaries — unique content not in arch spec |
| `architecture-decision-records.md` | ADR reference — consult when making decisions |
| `wireframe-v2.html` | Visual reference (open in browser) |
| `soft-bento-preview.html` | Design system reference (open in browser) |
| New implementation plan | (we'll create this next) |

### Archive (Phase 1 is done — these are historical)
| File | Reason |
|---|---|
| `ai-layer-architecture-v2.md` | 90% overlaps with architecture-spec + ADRs. The unique content (AI internals Mermaid diagram) is now in architecture-spec too |
| `Implementation_Plan_high_level.md` | Will be replaced by Phase 2 implementation plan |
| `phase-1-plan.md` | Phase 1 complete |
| `phase-1b-eval-matrix.md` | Phase 1 artifact |
| `phase-1b-reasoning-eval-report.md` | Phase 1 results (valuable history, not active reference) |
| `prompt_engineering_patterns.md` | Captured in memory + ADRs |
| `prototype-walkthrough.md` | Phase 1 onboarding doc |
| `eval_report_template.md` | Template, pull from archive if needed |
| `dev_log.md` | Historical notes |
| `open_question.md` | Empty file |
| `INDEX.md` | Becomes stale with fewer files |

**Result:** You go from ~15 files across 5 directories to **4 docs + 2 HTML files** in a flat structure. Claude Code reads architecture-spec as the primary reference, product-spec for features, ADRs for decisions.

Want me to do this?

content.content:
docs/01-plans/Implementation_Plan_high_level.md:41:**Scope:** Determined by what Phase 2 reveals — likely includes progressive SSE streaming (emitting typed events during the tool-use loop instead of after), cache layers for frequent KB queries, model tier routing for simple vs. complex turns, automated user profile extraction from conversation history, and scaling infrastructure. Decisions are made from evidence (usage patterns, latency data, cost breakdowns), not speculation.
docs/02-notes/architecture-decision-records.md:62:**Decision:** Use a compact structured user profile (Pydantic model, ~500 tokens, stored in PostgreSQL) injected into every system prompt. No vector search, no conversation history retrieval.
docs/02-notes/architecture-decision-records.md:70:- *Hybrid (profile + episodic RAG):* Only warranted if usage data shows episodic recall matters (e.g., "what did I cook last Thanksgiving?").
docs/02-notes/architecture-decision-records.md:263:**Trade-off:** Conversation history grows with each turn, increasing token costs. Mitigated by the context manager: Phase 2 uses simple truncation (last N turns + summary of older turns), Phase 3 upgrades to LLM-generated compression via `build_context()`.
docs/00-specs/ai-layer-architecture-v2.md:157:**Decision:** Use a compact, structured user profile document (Pydantic model stored in PostgreSQL) injected into every system prompt. No vector search, no conversation history retrieval.
docs/00-specs/ai-layer-architecture-v2.md:161:**Why:** The relevant user knowledge in the grocery domain is small (~500 tokens) and slowly-changing. A structured profile fits entirely in the system prompt without retrieval overhead. It's deterministic — the agent always sees the same profile, no retrieval quality variance. It also decouples the memory schema (what the agent reads) from the memory writer (what populates it), enabling a clean Phase 2 → Phase 3 evolution.
docs/00-specs/ai-layer-architecture-v2.md:166:- *Hybrid (profile + episodic RAG)* — structured profile for stable knowledge, RAG for episodic queries ("what did I cook last Thanksgiving?"). Only warranted if usage data shows episodic recall matters.
docs/00-specs/architecture-spec-v2.md:102:**Future scope:** A separate memory agent for cross-session _reasoning_ over conversation history (e.g., "what did I cook last Thanksgiving?") when saved content accumulates. Different tools, different prompt, different challenge — belongs in Phase 3 at earliest. The structured profile and read path remain unchanged when the memory agent is added.

content.text:
Yes, this decision is already recorded in multiple places. Here's where:

**Primary sources:**

1. **`architecture-spec-v2.md` line 102** — the clearest statement:
   > "A separate memory agent for cross-session *reasoning* over conversation history (e.g., 'what did I cook last Thanksgiving?') when saved content accumulates. Different tools, different prompt, different challenge — belongs in Phase 3 at earliest."

2. **`architecture-spec-v2.md` line 408** — Phase Alignment table:
   > Memory agent: "Not in scope" (Phase 1 & 2) → "Cross-session reasoning over history" (Phase 3)

3. **ADR-3** (`architecture-decision-records.md` line 62 + `ai-layer-architecture-v2.md` line 157) — the structured profile decision, which explicitly defers the hybrid approach:
   > "Hybrid (profile + episodic RAG): Only warranted if usage data shows episodic recall matters"

4. **`ai-layer-architecture-v2.md` line 161** — explains the decoupling:
   > "Decouples the memory schema (what the agent reads) from the memory writer (what populates it), enabling a clean Phase 2 → Phase 3 evolution."

**What's documented vs. what you described:**

The docs capture a **memory agent** for cross-session reasoning. But your description is broader — you're describing something more like a **unified context document** (like CLAUDE.md) that combines user history + chat sessions + saved plans + profile into one coherent context the agent always sees. That's a richer concept than what the docs currently describe, which focuses on episodic RAG queries.

Would you like me to save this broader vision as a project memory so it's captured for when you get to Phase 3? The existing docs cover the "when" (Phase 3) but not the full "what" as you just described it.

content.text:
# Plan Command

This command invokes the **planner** agent to create a comprehensive implementation plan before writing any code.

## What This Command Does

1. **Restate Requirements** - Clarify what needs to be built
2. **Identify Risks** - Surface potential issues and blockers
3. **Create Step Plan** - Break down implementation into phases
4. **Wait for Confirmation** - MUST receive user approval before proceeding

## When to Use

Use `/plan` when:
- Starting a new feature
- Making significant architectural changes
- Working on complex refactoring
- Multiple files/components will be affected
- Requirements are unclear or ambiguous

## How It Works

The planner agent will:

1. **Analyze the request** and restate requirements in clear terms
2. **Break down into phases** with specific, actionable steps
3. **Identify dependencies** between components
4. **Assess risks** and potential blockers
5. **Estimate complexity** (High/Medium/Low)
6. **Present the plan** and WAIT for your explicit confirmation

## Example Usage

```
User: /plan I need to add real-time notifications when markets resolve

Agent (planner):
# Implementation Plan: Real-Time Market Resolution Notifications

## Requirements Restatement
- Send notifications to users when markets they're watching resolve
- Support multiple notification channels (in-app, email, webhook)
- Ensure notifications are delivered reliably
- Include market outcome and user's position result

## Implementation Phases

### Phase 1: Database Schema
- Add notifications table with columns: id, user_id, market_id, type, status, created_at
- Add user_notification_preferences table for channel preferences
- Create indexes on user_id and market_id for performance

### Phase 2: Notification Service
- Create notification service in lib/notifications.ts
- Implement notification queue using BullMQ/Redis
- Add retry logic for failed deliveries
- Create notification templates

### Phase 3: Integration Points
- Hook into market resolution logic (when status changes to "resolved")
- Query all users with positions in market
- Enqueue notifications for each user

### Phase 4: Frontend Components
- Create NotificationBell component in header
- Add NotificationList modal
- Implement real-time updates via WebSocket or polling
- Add notification preferences page

## Dependencies
- Redis (for queue)
- Email service (SendGrid/Resend)
- WebSocket or polling for real-time updates

## Risks
- HIGH: Email deliverability (SPF/DKIM required)
- MEDIUM: Performance with 1000+ users per market
- MEDIUM: Notification spam if markets resolve frequently
- LOW: Real-time subscription overhead

## Estimated Complexity: MEDIUM
- Backend: 4-6 hours
- Frontend: 3-4 hours
- Testing: 2-3 hours
- Total: 9-13 hours

**WAITING FOR CONFIRMATION**: Proceed with this plan? (yes/no/modify)
```

## Important Notes

**CRITICAL**: The planner agent will **NOT** write any code until you explicitly confirm the plan with "yes" or "proceed" or similar affirmative response.

If you want changes, respond with:
- "modify: [your changes]"
- "different approach: [alternative]"
- "skip phase 2 and do phase 3 first"

## Integration with Other Commands

After planning:
- Use `/tdd` to implement with test-driven development
- Use `/build-fix` if build errors occur
- Use `/code-review` to review completed implementation

## Related Agents

This command invokes the `planner` agent provided by ECC.

For manual installs, the source file lives at:
`agents/planner.md`


ARGUMENTS: based on the architecture we just write, discuss with me on the implementation plan. 

here is the plan I am thinking:

The development plan I am thinking: several worktree working at the same time:

1. One for front end following this process

   ```tex    
   **Claude Code stage 1: static HTML pass.** Point it at the design doc + wireframe + Recipes reference, ask it to generate the other 6 screens as static HTML matching the Soft Bento system. You review each, correct visually, iterate. No JS, no framework — just HTML/CSS so review is fast and cheap.
   
   **Stage 2: convert to React components.** Once all 7 screens look right as static HTML, Claude Code translates them into React + TS components with typed props. Still mock data, still no backend.
   
   **Stage 3: wire mock data + state machine.** The `IDLE → LOADING → STREAMING → COMPLETE` state machine from your architecture spec, with hardcoded mock responses instead of real SSE. You validate the full flow clicks through correctly.
   
   **Stage 4: connect to real AI layer.** SSE client, real `/chat` endpoint, typed evling.
One for the remaining task in AI layer

One for backend

One for accessing the knowledge base data and data generation
Let me re-read the updated architecture spec and the current prototype code to ground this discussion properly.

content.text:
Good. Now let me think through your plan and give you my analysis.

---

## My take on your plan

Your 4-worktree parallel approach is smart — it lets you develop independently and merge when ready. But I see some **dependency ordering issues** and **missing pieces** that we should discuss.

### The dependency graph

```
                    KB Data (Worktree 4)
                        │
                        ▼
    AI Layer (Worktree 2) ──────► Backend (Worktree 3)
         │                              │
         │                              ▼
         │                     API contract (types + endpoints)
         │                              │
         ▼                              ▼
    Eval suite still works      Frontend Stage 4 (Worktree 1)
```

The critical path is: **KB data → AI layer (async rewrite) → Backend (FastAPI + SSE) → Frontend connects**. Your frontend stages 1-3 can run in parallel with everything else, but stage 4 is blocked by the backend being ready.

### What I'd adjust

**Your Worktree 1 (Frontend) — the 4-stage plan is solid.** One refinement: between Stage 2 and Stage 3, you need the **shared TypeScript types** (SSE event payloads, API request/response shapes). These should come from the backend API contract, not be invented on the frontend. I'd add a "Stage 2.5: define shared types from architecture spec" step, or have the backend worktree produce these types first.

**Your Worktree 2 (AI Layer remaining tasks)** — what's actually left?
- Phase 1c item 1: OQ-3 model comparison (run eval suite against cheaper model)
- Phase 1c item 2: Add 3-5 vegetarian/vegan recipes to KB
- Async rewrite of orchestrator (sync → `AsyncOpenAI`)
- Adapt tool handlers from JSON files → SQLite queries

The first two are quick (1c cleanup). The async rewrite + SQLite migration are the real work and they're tightly coupled with the backend.

**Your Worktree 3 (Backend)** — this is the most complex worktree. It needs:
- FastAPI app structure, routers, middleware
- PostgreSQL schema + connection (sessions, users, saved content)
- SQLite integration for KB
- `/chat` SSE endpoint wrapping the async orchestrator
- Session CRUD, saved content CRUD
- Mock auth middleware
- Context manager (turn history + truncation)

**Your Worktree 4 (KB data)** — this is a prerequisite for the backend's SQLite layer. The data exists as JSON; it needs SQLite schema design + migration script. Plus the vegetarian recipe gap from Phase 1c.

### The problem I see

**Worktrees 2 and 3 are too entangled to be separate.** The async orchestrator rewrite IS the backend's core — you can't build the `/chat` endpoint without it. And the tool handlers switching from JSON to SQLite IS the backend's data layer. I'd merge them.

### My proposed restructure

**4 worktrees, but reorganized:**

| Worktree | Focus | Blocks on |
|---|---|---|

Good — let me think through the timeline and Docker questions together, since they're connected.

### Question 2: Development sequence options

I need to understand one thing first: **how many hours per week are you spending on this?** And is the "beginning of May" deadline hard (professor submission) or soft (personal goal)?

This matters because the options look very different at 10 hrs/week vs 30 hrs/week.

That said, here are three approaches:

---

**Option A: Fully sequential (lowest risk, slowest)**

```
Week 1:     Phase 1c cleanup (OQ-3 + veggie recipes)
Week 2-3:   KB + Data (SQLite schema, migration)
Week 3-5:   Backend + AI Layer (FastAPI, async orchestrator, SSE, PostgreSQL)
Week 4-6:   Frontend stages 1-3 (can overlap with late backend)
Week 6-7:   Frontend stage 4 (connect to real backend)
Week 7:     Integration testing, Docker Compose finalized
```

**Pros:** Simple. One thing at a time. No merge conflicts. Easy to track progress.
**Cons:** Slow. ~7 weeks. No parallelism. You're idle on frontend while backend isn't ready (or vice versa).
**Best for:** Very limited hours per week, or if you want to deeply learn each layer before moving on.

---

**Option B: Two-track parallel (balanced risk/speed) — recommended**

```
Track A (you, primary):          Track B (secondary/Claude Code):
─────────────────────────────    ────────────────────────────────
Week 1: Phase 1c cleanup         Week 1: Frontend Stage 1 (static HTML)
Week 2: KB + SQLite migration    Week 2: Frontend Stage 2 (React components)
Week 3-4: Backend + AI Layer     Week 3: Frontend Stage 3 (mock state machine)
Week 5: Integration (connect)    Week 4: Frontend Stage 3 cont'd
Week 5-6: Testing + polish       Week 5-6: Frontend Stage 4 (real SSE)
```

**Pros:** ~5-6 weeks. Backend (your focus/interest) gets your full attention. Frontend progresses in parallel via Claude Code with your review. The frontend stages 1-3 need zero backend — they're purely visual + mock data. By the time frontend needs the real backend (Stage 4), it's ready.

**Cons:** You need to define the API contract (TypeScript types for SSE events + API endpoints) early — Week 2 at

For the development sequence options, I think you are totally in the wrong direction. I heard the developers @CLAUDE.md Coding use six worktrees at the same time. What you are suggesting is basically the programmer without agentic coding, so please search the new patterns. I'm thinking that for different worktrees we should use a contract as the source of truth, like API documents or schema as the communication contract, to treat different worktrees as different microservices that are happening independently from each other. The contract here is a bond of them, and the main branch should be the orchestration for the merging and managing part. Please do some research, then give me real options -- don't simply generate from the training data you have.

here is some reference for you:
## Worktree Orchestration — Summary

**Question:** How to coordinate 4 parallel worktrees (frontend, AI layer, backend, KB) so interface changes (SSE events, tool contracts, KB schema) propagate correctly and each Claude Code agent learns about them at the right time?

**Core insight:** Worktrees solve file conflicts, not interface drift. Need a separate coordination mechanism for shared contracts.

**Recommendation: Contracts directory + orchestrator worktree**

1. **`contracts/` in `main` as single source of truth:**
   - `sse_events.py` — Pydantic models for all SSE event types
   - `tools.py` — 6 tool input/output schemas
   - `openapi.yaml` — generated from FastAPI, committed
   - `kb_schema.sql` — SQLite DDL
   - `CHANGELOG.md` — one-line dated entries for breaking changes

2. **Update protocol:**
   - Contract changes go in their own small PR to `main`, merged immediately, no implementation mixed in
   - Each worktree rebases on `main` before each session
   - Each worktree's `CLAUDcts the agent to read `contracts/CHANGELOG.md` for entries newer than last session

3. **Per-worktree `CLAUDE.md`** pins each agent to its slice: what it owns, what it imports from `contracts/`, what it must not edit.

4. **Orchestrator pattern:** Stay in `main` as the coordination hub. You merge contract PRs, dispatch tasks, review outputs. Avoids context-switching across 4 live agent conversations (practical limit is 3–5 parallel worktrees before overhead dominates).

**Sequencing:** Week 1 sequential in `main` to draft `contracts/`. Week 2+ parallel: AI layer + KB + backend skeleton + frontend Stages 1–2 (no contract dependency). Frontend Stage 3 is the first hard sync point — needs SSE contract frozen.

**Why not status-file-only coordination:** markdown is easy to skip and easy to misread. Schemas in code fail loudly when violated.

**References:**
- [Contract-first parallel development (Evil Martians)](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/api-contracts-and-everything-i-wish-i-knew-a-frontend-survuide)
- [Claude Code worktrees + per-worktree CLAUDE.md (MindStudio)](https://www.mindstudio.ai/blog/claude-code-git-worktrees-parallel-branches)
- [Parallel worktrees coordination pattern (SpillwaveSolutions skill)](https://github.com/spillwavesolutions/parallel-worktrees)
- [Claude Code worktree docs](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows)

content.text:
Perfect. Now I have the comprehensive official documentation. Let me address your five questions with precise, documentation-backed guidance.

## How Claude Code Worktrees Work: Complete Guide

### 1. Launching Multiple Agents in Separate Git Worktrees

There are **two distinct approaches**, and the terminology matters:

**Approach A: Git Worktrees with Manual Session Management**
```bash
# Create and launch independent Claude Code sessions in separate worktrees
claude --worktree feature-auth
claude --worktree bugfix-123
claude --worktree  # auto-generates a name like "bright-running-fox"
```

Each worktree:
- Creates a new directory at `.claude/worktrees/<name>/`
- Branches from your remote's default branch (`origin/HEAD`)
- Creates a branch named `worktree-<name>`
- Runs a completely independent Claude Code session—**no automatic coordination**
- Shares the same `.git` database (efficient, no duplication)

**Approach B: Agent Teams (Experimental, Coordinated)**
For parallel agents that **actively communicate and share a task list**, use Agent Teams instead:
```bash
export CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS=1
claude
# Then ask: "Create an agent team with 3 teammates to work on X"
```

Agent Teams provide:
- Shared task list with pending/in-progress/completed states
- Direct inter-agent messaging (not just reporting back to lead)
- Automatic task dependency management
- Lead session that coordinates and synthesizes

**Key difference**: Worktrees = manual sessions you control; Agent Teams = orchestrated parallel execution with the lead agent managing coordination.

---

### 2. Per-Worktree CLAUDE.md Configuration

Worktrees **automatically scope each agent to its directory**, so CLAUDE.md works perfectly for isolation:

```
project-root/
├── CLAUDE.md                      # Main instructions (loaded in main worktree)
├── .claude/
│   └── worktrees/
│       ├── feature-auth/
│       │   ├── CLAUDE.md          # Auto-loaded for agent in this worktree
│       │   └── src/
│       ├── bugfix-123/
│       │   ├── CLAUDE.md          # Different instructions for this agent
│       │   └── src/
```

**How it works** (from docs):
- Each Claude Code instance reads **only files in its worktree directory**
- When you `cd .claude/worktrees/feature-auth && claude`, it loads that worktree's CLAUDE.md
- Won't accidentally edit files in a sibling worktree
- Per-worktree CLAUDE.md can pin that agent to a specific scope/role

**Recommended pattern**:
```markdown
# .claude/worktrees/feature-auth/CLAUDE.md
## Scope
You are working on authentication refactor (PR #456).
Only touch src/auth/ and tests/auth/.
Never modify database migrations or API routes.

## Architecture context
[Local auth-specific context]
```

---

### 3. Coordinating Shared Contracts Between Worktrees

The official pattern is **"orchestrator stays in main"** — use the orchestrator pattern:

**Architecture:**
```
Main branch (Orchestrator session)
├── Reads shared CLAUDE.md with API contracts
├── Launches subagents or worktree agents
├── Collects results from each
└── Synthesizes into main

Worktree #1 (Feature A)
├── Implements feature-specific code
└── Reports back to orchestrator

Worktree #2 (Feature B)
├── Implements separate feature
└── Reports back to orchestrator
```

**Shared contracts live in the main CLAUDE.md**:
```markdown
# Root CLAUDE.md (checked in, read by all agents)

## Shared API Contracts

### Tool Interface (All agents implement these)
- `analyze_pcsv(ingredients: List[str]) -> PCSVAnalysis`
- `search_recipes(query: str) -> List[Recipe]`

### Data schema
User profile shape (Pydantic model)
Event types for SSE streaming

## Architectural decisions
- [Links to your ADRs in docs/]
- Reason for SQLite vs PostgreSQL split
- Why PCV gap analysis is deterministic, not LLM-driven
```

**For Agent Teams specifically** (from docs):
> "Every teammate reads the project's CLAUDE.md on startup, so interface contracts, architectural decisions, and coding conventions can be shared there so teammates stay aligned without constant messaging."

This means **even with parallel agents, put contracts in CLAUDE.md** rather than trying to sync files between worktrees.

---

### 4. Practical Limits on Parallel Worktrees

**Maximum parallel agents: 7 parallel Claude Code instances** (from search results).

However, practical limits are lower:

| Metric | Practical Limit |
|--------|-----------------|
| **Concurrent worktree sessions you launch** | 5-7 (you're controlling each manually) |
| **Agent Teams teammates** | 3-5 optimal; up to 16 supported |
| **Subagent worktrees** | Unlimited (auto-cleanup, auto-spawned within single lead session) |
| **Context window per agent** | Standard 200K (Haiku 4.5) |

**Reasons to stay lower:**
- Token costs scale linearly with each active agent
- Coordination overhead increases (especially with manual worktrees)
- File conflict risk when >3 agents edit overlapping modules
- CPU/memory contention on your machine

**From docs**: "For research, review, and new feature work, the extra tokens are usually worthwhile. For routine tasks, a single session is more cost-effective."

---

### 5. The "Orchestrator Stays in Main" Pattern

This is the **recommended production pattern** for your SGA V2 project:

**Setup:**
```bash
# Stay in main
cd /path/to/SGA_V2
claude --name orchestrator
```

**Your instructions to the orchestrator**:
```
I'm building SGA V2. Use worktrees to parallelize:
- Worktree 1: Implement FastAPI backend (api-backend)
- Worktree 2: Build React SPA frontend (frontend-spa)
- Worktree 3: Create SQLite KB schema (kb-schema)

Each agent should implement their component independently, reporting
to me (the orchestrator) with:
1. API contracts they're implementing
2. Data model assumptions
3. Integration points with other components

I'll synthesize PRs and handle merging.
```

**How it works:**
1. **Orchestrator session** (main branch) reads root CLAUDE.md with shared contracts
2. **Ask Claude** to create worktrees and launch agents (or use Agent Teams with `isolation: worktree`)
3. **Each agent** works independently in parallel, bound to their scope
4. **Agents report back** with code changes, design decisions, integration points
5. **Orchestrator** reviews, adjusts shared contracts if needed, coordinates merges
6. **Cleanup**: worktrees auto-remove if no changes; you keep branches with commits

**For SGA V2 specifically**, this means:
- Root CLAUDE.md defines your 7 tools (analyze_pcsv, search_recipes, etc.) as shared contracts
- Each worktree agent specializes: one on API layer, one on knowledge base, one on React frontend
- No agent edits overlapping files without coordination through the orchestrator
- When done, orchestrator merges branches into main via PRs

---

## Worktree Lifecycle: Setup and Cleanup

**Creation:**
```bash
claude --worktree feature-auth
```
- Creates `.claude/worktrees/feature-auth/`
- Runs `git worktree add` under the hood
- Branches from `origin/HEAD` (your default remote branch)
- Creates branch `worktree-feature-auth`

**Cleanup (automatic):**
- **No changes**: worktree and branch removed on exit
- **Changes exist**: Claude prompts you to keep or remove
- **Subagent worktrees**: auto-removed after `cleanupPeriodDays` if no modifications

**Manual cleanup** (if needed):
```bash
git worktree list
git worktree remove .claude/worktrees/feature-auth
```

**Pro tip**: Add to `.gitignore`:
```
.claude/worktrees/
```

---

## Recommended Configuration for Your SGA V2 Project

Based on your CLAUDE.md showing "Phase 2 target" with backend/frontend split:

```json
// .claude/settings.json
{
  "permissions": {
    "defaultMode": "plan"  // Safe mode for parallel agents
  },
  "env": {
    "CLAUDE_CODE_EXPERIMENTAL_AGENT_TEAMS": "1"  // For coordinated teams
  },
  "hooks": {
    "WorktreeCreate": [{...}]  // Optional: custom worktree setup (copy .env, etc.)
  }
}
```

Then from main:
```bash
claude -n sga-orchestrator
# Prompt: "Create an agent team to build SGA V2 Phase 2..."
```

---

## Sources:
- [Claude Code Common Workflows - Worktrees](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows.md#run-parallel-claude-code-sessions-with-git-worktrees)
- [Claude Code Agent Teams Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/agent-teams.md)
- [Claude Code Subagents Documentation](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/sub-agents.md)
- [Mastering Parallel Agent Development in Claude Code](https://claudelab.net/en/articles/claude-code/claude-code-parallel-development-mastery)
- [How to Run Claude Code Agents in Parallel](https://towardsdatascience.com/how-to-run-claude-code-agents-in-parallel/)
- [Mastering Git Worktrees with Claude Code](https://medium.com/@dtunai/mastering-git-worktrees-with-claude-code-parallel-development-workflow-41dc91e645fe)

Based on my web search and codebase exploration, here's a comprehensive report on contract-first parallel development patterns:

## Key Findings

### 1. **Contracts Directory as Single Source of Truth**

The pattern works by establishing the API contract **before any code is written**:

- **Contract Definition**: Use OpenAPI specs, Pydantic models, or GraphQL schemas as the canonical spec that exists in version control
- **Shared Ownership**: Frontend, backend, QA, and design all contribute to the contract before implementation begins
- **Central Git Repository**: The .git folder serves as the single source of truth when using git worktrees—all worktrees share one .git database
- **One Place to Live**: The spec (whether OpenAPI or Pydantic) exists in one canonical location, committed to the repository

**Practical Benefit**: Frontend and backend teams work in parallel from day one, using mock servers and real implementations against the same specification, eliminating "waiting for backend" bottlenecks.

### 2. **Update Protocol: Contract PRs → Rebase Before PR**

The coordination workflow follows this sequence:

1. **Contract Changes First**: Task zero is always contract updates (PR merged to main before implementation)
2. **Feature Development**: Both frontend and backend start from the updated contract simultaneously
3. **Rebase Before PR**: Before opening a PR for feature implementation, run `git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main`
4. **Worktree Rebase**: In worktree-based workflows, you don't need to checkout main—rebase from origin directly within your worktree
5. **Clean History**: Rebasing keeps project history clean and minimizes merge conflicts

This ensures all parallel work is built on the latest contract, preventing integration surprises.

### 3. **Per-Worktree CLAUDE.md Files for Agent Scope**

Each worktree has an independent `CLAUDE.md` that scopes the agent's responsibilities:

- **No Cascade from Parent**: Guidelines in the parent worktree CLAUDE.md do NOT automatically apply to child worktrees—each is independent
- **Task-Specific Instructions**: Each worktree's CLAUDE.md loads at session start with:
  - What the agent owns (the feature/component)
  - What it imports (dependencies, shared contracts)
  - What it must NOT edit (other domains, core contracts)
- **Isolation via Frontmatter**: Subagents use `isolation: worktree` to get auto-cleaned isolated working directories
- **Session Persistence**: CLAUDE.md provides persistent context, but there's no memory of previous sessions unless explicitly added to the file

**Best Practice**: Run `/init` in each new worktree session to ensure Claude Code is properly oriented with the codebase and has the correct task scope.

### 4. **Real Examples of Teams Using This Pattern**

#### Claude Code Native Support
- **Built-in Worktree Support**: Claude Code Desktop (and now CLI) has native git worktree support for parallel agents
- **Max 7 Parallel Agents**: Claude Code can orchestrate up to 7 simultaneous agents across different worktrees
- **Auto-Cleanup**: Subagents automatically clean up their worktrees when finished without changes

#### Community Implementations
Several teams document this pattern:
- **Steve Kinney's Course**: "Using Git Worktrees for Parallel AI Development" covers coordination strategies
- **Medium Articles**: Multiple reports from teams running 3–5 parallel worktrees as practical upper bound (beyond that, context-switching overhead increases)
- **pnpm + Worktrees**: pnpm's global virtual store pattern allows shared dependencies across worktrees while maintaining isolation

#### Evil Martians Approach
- **Contract-First Workflow**: Frontend and backend teams have equal access and shared responsibility for the API contract
- **Parallel Feature Ship**: Features ship faster because both sides work simultaneously against one spec, not sequentially
- **Single Source of Truth**: One place where the API contract lives—all teams build to it

## In This Repository

Your SGA V2 project's `CLAUDE.md` defines a single conversational agent with clear responsibilities. To apply contract-first parallel development here, you would:

1. **Create a `contracts/` directory** with:
   - OpenAPI spec (or Pydantic model definitions) for the `/chat` SSE endpoint
   - SQLite schema definitions (recipes, PCSV mappings, products, substitutions)
   - Pydantic models for UserProfile, RecipeCard, SSE events, etc.

2. **Set up per-feature worktrees** (one for backend tools, one for frontend UI, one for KB schema):
   - Each gets its own worktree-specific CLAUDE.md defining what it owns
   - Backend worktree: "Implements tool handlers, imports from contracts/"
   - Frontend worktree: "Builds UI components, imports event types from contracts/"
   - KB worktree: "Curates SQLite schema, exports to contracts/ → other worktrees rebase"

3. **Protocol**: Contract PRs merge to main first, then feature branches rebase and proceed in parallel.

---

## Sources

- [Mastering Git Worktrees with Claude Code for Parallel Development](https://medium.com/@dtunai/mastering-git-worktrees-with-claude-code-for-parallel-development-workflow-41dc91e645fe)
- [Mastering Parallel Agent Development in Claude Code](https://claudelab.net/en/articles/claude-code/claude-code-parallel-development-mastery)
- [API contracts and everything I wish I knew—Evil Martians](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/api-contracts-and-everything-i-wish-i-knew-a-frontend-survival-guide)
- [Contract-First API Development: The Spec as Executable Truth](https://devguide.dev/blog/contract-first-api-development)
- [Contract Shock Therapy—Evil Martians](https://evilmartians.com/chronicles/contract-shock-therapy-the-way-to-api-first-documentation-bliss)
- [Using Git Worktrees for Concurrent Development](https://www.kenmuse.com/blog/using-git-worktrees-for-concurrent-development/)
- [Using Git Worktrees for Parallel AI Development](https://stevekinney.com/courses/ai-development/git-worktrees)
- [pnpm + Git Worktrees for Multi-Agent Development](https://pnpm.io/next/git-worktrees)
- [Claude Code Common Workflows](https://code.claude.com/docs/en/common-workflows)
- [Git Worktree Documentation](https://git-scm.com/docs/git-worktree)
